diagrams.title: Longitudinal Zonation of a River
diagrams.description: A schematic showing the transition from headwaters (narrow, rocky, fast-flowing) through middle course (wider, moderate flow) to lower course (broad, slow, with floodplain), including organisms typical to each zone.
elements: Crenal zone at source with spring
Narrow headwaters with boulders and riffles
Middle course with pools and riffles
Wide lower course with meanders and floodplain
Estuary with tidal influence
Arrows showing flow direction
Icons of typical organisms: trout in headwaters, bass in middle, carp in lower
Temperature gradient arrow (cold to warm)
Width increasing downstream
diagrams.purpose: Visualize how physical conditions and biological communities change along river length, fundamental to understanding river continuum concept

diagrams.title: Oxygen Sag Curve
diagrams.description: Graph showing dissolved oxygen (DO) levels versus distance from a pollution source, illustrating the Streeter-Phelps oxygen sag phenomenon.
elements: X-axis: Distance downstream from pollution source
Y-axis: DO concentration (mg/L)
Saturation line at 8-10 mg/L
DO curve starting at saturation, dropping sharply after pollution input
Critical point at minimum DO (around 4 mg/L indicated)
Recovery curve rising back toward saturation
Labels: deoxygenation zone, critical point, reaeration zone
Pollution source point marked
BOD curve (inverted) shown for comparison
diagrams.purpose: Understand how organic pollution depletes oxygen and how reaeration restores it; critical for water quality management and pollution control

diagrams.title: River Cross-Section and Hyporheic Zone
diagrams.description: Cross-sectional view of a river showing surface water, bed sediments, and the hyporheic zone where groundwater and surface water mix.
elements: Water surface with flowing water column
Riverbed with substrate (gravel, sand, silt)
Hyporheic zone beneath bed showing pore spaces
Groundwater table on sides
Arrows showing water exchange between surface and hyporheic zone
Organisms in each zone: fish in water column, invertebrates in substrate, special hyporheic species
capillary fringe marked
Riparian zone on banks
diagrams.purpose: Illustrate the three-dimensional nature of river ecosystems including the important hyporheic zone that serves as refuge, nutrient processing area, and habitat for many species
